1. Diffusion-Enhanced Test-Time Adaptation with Text and Image Augmentation (International Journal of Computer Vision 2024, cites: 6)
   Existing test-time prompt tuning (TPT) methods focus on single-modality data, primarily enhancing images and using confidence ratings to filter out inaccurate images. However, while image generation models can produce visually diverse images, single-modality data enhancement techniques still fail to capture the comprehensive knowledge provided by different modalities. Additionally, we note that the performance of TPT-based methods drops significantly when the number of augmented images is limited, which is not unusual given the computational expense of generative augmentation. To address these issues, we introduce IT3A\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$\text {IT}^{3}\text {A}$$\end{document}, a novel test-time adaptation method that utilizes a pre-trained generative model for multi-modal augmentation of each test sample from unknown new domains. By combining augmented data from pre-trained vision and language models, we enhance the ability of the model to adapt to unknown new test data. Additionally, to ensure that key semantics are accurately retained when generating various visual and text enhancements, we employ cosine similarity filtering between the logits of the enhanced images and text with the original test data. This process allows us to filter out some spurious augmentation and inadequate combinations. To leverage the diverse enhancements provided by the generation model across different modals, we have replaced prompt tuning with an adapter for greater flexibility in utilizing text templates. Our experiments on the test datasets with distribution shifts and domain gaps show that in a zero-shot setting, IT3A\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$\text {IT}^{3}\text {A}$$\end{document} outperforms state-of-the-art test-time prompt tuning methods with a 5.50% increase in accuracy.

2. Test-Time Adaptive Object Detection with Foundation Model (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 2)
   In recent years, test-time adaptive object detection has attracted increasing attention due to its unique advantages in online domain adaptation, which aligns more closely with real-world application scenarios. However, existing approaches heavily rely on source-derived statistical characteristics while making the strong assumption that the source and target domains share an identical category space. In this paper, we propose the first foundation model-powered test-time adaptive object detection method that eliminates the need for source data entirely and overcomes traditional closed-set limitations. Specifically, we design a Multi-modal Prompt-based Mean-Teacher framework for vision-language detector-driven test-time adaptation, which incorporates text and visual prompt tuning to adapt both language and vision representation spaces on the test data in a parameter-efficient manner. Correspondingly, we propose a Test-time Warm-start strategy tailored for the visual prompts to effectively preserve the representation capability of the vision branch. Furthermore, to guarantee high-quality pseudo-labels in every test batch, we maintain an Instance Dynamic Memory (IDM) module that stores high-quality pseudo-labels from previous test samples, and propose two novel strategies-Memory Enhancement and Memory Hallucination-to leverage IDM's high-quality instances for enhancing original predictions and hallucinating images without available pseudo-labels, respectively. Extensive experiments on cross-corruption and cross-dataset benchmarks demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods, and can adapt to arbitrary cross-domain and cross-category target data. Code is available at https://github.com/gaoyingjay/ttaod_foundation.

3. CLIP's Visual Embedding Projector is a Few-shot Cornucopia ( 2024, cites: 1)
   We introduce ProLIP, a simple and architecture-agnostic method for adapting contrastively pretrained vision-language models, such as CLIP, to few-shot classification. ProLIP fine-tunes the vision encoder's projection matrix with Frobenius norm regularization on its deviation from the pretrained weights. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on 11 few-shot classification benchmarks under both ``few-shot validation''and ``validation-free''settings. Moreover, by rethinking the non-linear CLIP-Adapter through ProLIP's lens, we design a Regularized Linear Adapter (RLA) that performs better, requires no hyperparameter tuning, is less sensitive to learning rate values, and offers an alternative to ProLIP in black-box scenarios where model weights are inaccessible. Beyond few-shot classification, ProLIP excels in cross-dataset transfer, domain generalization, base-to-new class generalization, and test-time adaptation--where it outperforms prompt tuning while being an order of magnitude faster to train. Code is available at https://github.com/astra-vision/ProLIP .

1. Test-Time Adaptation via Cache Personalization for Facial Expression Recognition in Videos ( 2026, cites: 1)
   Facial expression recognition (FER) in videos requires model personalization to capture the considerable variations across subjects. Vision-language models (VLMs) offer strong transfer to downstream tasks through image-text alignment, but their performance can still degrade under inter-subject distribution shifts. Personalizing models using test-time adaptation (TTA) methods can mitigate this challenge. However, most state-of-the-art TTA methods rely on unsupervised parameter optimization, introducing computational overhead that is impractical in many real-world applications. This paper introduces TTA through Cache Personalization (TTA-CaP), a cache-based TTA method that enables cost-effective (gradient-free) personalization of VLMs for video FER. Prior cache-based TTA methods rely solely on dynamic memories that store test samples, which can accumulate errors and drift due to noisy pseudo-labels. TTA-CaP leverages three coordinated caches: a personalized source cache that stores source-domain prototypes, a positive target cache that accumulates reliable subject-specific samples, and a negative target cache that stores low-confidence cases as negative samples to reduce the impact of noisy pseudo-labels. Cache updates and replacement are controlled by a tri-gate mechanism based on temporal stability, confidence, and consistency with the personalized cache. Finally, TTA-CaP refines predictions through fusion of embeddings, yielding refined representations that support temporally stable video-level predictions. Our experiments on three challenging video FER datasets, BioVid, StressID, and BAH, indicate that TTA-CaP can outperform state-of-the-art TTA methods under subject-specific and environmental shifts, while maintaining low computational and memory overhead for real-world deployment.

2. Semantic Anchor Transport: Robust Test-Time Adaptation for Vision-Language Models ( 2024, cites: 2)
   Large pre-trained vision-language models (VLMs), such as CLIP, have shown unprecedented zero-shot performance across a wide range of tasks. Nevertheless, these models may be unreliable under distributional shifts, as their performance is significantly degraded. In this work, we investigate how to efficiently utilize class text information to mitigate distribution drifts encountered by VLMs during inference. In particular, we propose generating pseudo-labels for the noisy test-time samples by aligning visual embeddings with reliable, text-based semantic anchors. Specifically, to maintain the regular structure of the dataset properly, we formulate the problem as a batch-wise label assignment, which is efficiently solved using Optimal Transport. Our method, Semantic Anchor Transport (SAT), utilizes such pseudo-labels as supervisory signals for test-time adaptation, yielding a principled cross-modal alignment solution. Moreover, SAT further leverages heterogeneous textual clues, with a multi-template distillation approach that replicates multi-view contrastive learning strategies in unsupervised representation learning without incurring additional computational complexity. Extensive experiments on multiple popular test-time adaptation benchmarks presenting diverse complexity empirically show the superiority of SAT, achieving consistent performance gains over recent state-of-the-art methods, yet being computationally efficient.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Vision-language models trained on web data degrade when images
come from a shifted distribution like medical or satellite;
how can we maintain cross-modal alignment under unseen domain
shift without target-domain labels?


### Proposed method
We propose COMPASS, a test-time and continual, label-free adaptation layer that preserves vision–language alignment under unseen domain shift (e.g., medical, satellite) without target-domain labels. Starting from a frozen web-trained VLM (e.g., CLIP-like), we keep the text encoder fixed and adapt only a small set of vision-side modules: (i) a lightweight visual prompt front-end (pixel-level or early-feature prompts) and (ii) a factorized adapter that decomposes the visual representation into a shared, alignment-preserving pathway and a domain-specific modulation pathway. An exponential-moving-average (EMA) teacher of the full model anchors the original cross-modal geometry and prevents drift.

The domain-binding front-end couples two mechanisms. First, a learnable visual prompt bank is injected before or into the first few vision blocks, nudging inputs from the target domain toward the web pretraining manifold. Second, an amortized inference head estimates a per-image domain code z_dom that modulates intermediate features via a small FiLM/LoRA adapter stack, while a shared pathway passes gradients only through the adapters and prompts (the backbone remains frozen). We place a simple Gaussian prior over z_dom and regularize with a KL term so the domain-specific modulation remains compact and does not entangle with the shared alignment space. Both prompts and adapters are updated online, while the z_dom prior is refined slowly across the stream.

Unsupervised alignment is maintained with three complementary signals. (1) Self-captioned contrastive alignment: for each target image, the VLM (or a small caption head) generates K candidate captions; we keep high-confidence text anchors and optimize an InfoNCE loss to increase image–text similarity for the top caption and decrease it for hard negatives (including other candidates and a fixed bank of generic web prompts). (2) Cross-view consistency: two strong augmentations of the same image must produce similar similarities over the caption bank, stabilizing the image embedding without labels. (3) Entropy shaping: we minimize entropy of the image–text similarity distribution over the candidate bank to produce confident, clustered alignments while avoiding collapse with temperature and teacher constraints.

To avoid catastrophic forgetting and preserve cross-modal geometry, we use a teacher–student consistency term: the student’s similarity matrix over a small replay buffer of web images and a fixed set of web text anchors is matched to the EMA teacher. We further add a lightweight domain discriminator on shared visual features with a gradient-reversal layer to keep the shared pathway domain-invariant; the domain-specific modulation absorbs style/content shift. Updates are uncertainty-weighted: per-sample confidence scores downweight noisy self-captions and limit prompt/adapter drift. Only prompts, adapters, z_dom inference, and the domain discriminator are trained; the text encoder, visual backbone, and the EMA teacher’s main weights remain frozen.

Deployment: as target images stream in, we run a few inner-loop updates per batch with small learning rates and an uncertainty-guided EMA on the prompt bank. We cap compute by freezing deeper adapters, and apply early stopping when teacher–student discrepancy stops improving. At inference, the adapted prompts/adapters are used with the frozen text encoder; no target labels or paired texts are required, and the method remains open-vocabulary for downstream zero-shot queries.

### Rationale
Cross-modal degradation under domain shift stems from visual features drifting away from the text-aligned manifold. By freezing the text encoder and constraining adaptation to small visual prompts and domain-specific adapters, we retain the shared alignment while granting just enough capacity to absorb style/content shift. The domain-factorized design explicitly separates a stable, alignment-preserving pathway from a compact domain modulation controlled by a prior, reducing the chance that adaptation breaks the learned image–text geometry.

Because no labels are available, we rely on self-captioning and open-vocabulary text banks to create pseudo text anchors, but guard against error amplification with uncertainty-weighted updates, cross-view consistency, and teacher–student constraints on a web replay buffer. Adversarial alignment further nudges shared features toward domain invariance, while uncertainty-aware prompt updates keep compute low and adaptation stable. Together, these ingredients maintain cross-modal alignment in new domains without target supervision.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [IR_REC] GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation (2007.02747)
   problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
   method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

2. [IR_REC] Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition (2505.07166)
   problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
   method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

3. [LLM_NLP] Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement (2402.11436)
   problem: LLM self-refine and self-rewarding pipelines can optimize toward falsely perceived improvements due to self-evaluation bias, causing quality to stagnate or degrade.
   method:  Empirically compare LLM self-evaluations against reference-based BLEURT and human MQM across six LLMs, four languages, and three tasks, showing universal self-bias that amplifies over iterations; in self-rewarding, increasing the candidate pool size further amplifies bias. Analyze what changes after self-refine with UniEval and style-paraphrasing experiments, finding perceived gains are mainly in fluency/understandability and style alignment rather than actual task objectives, explaining the divergence between self-estimated and true performance.

4. [CV] KOALA: A Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity (2107.03331)
   problem: Momentum improves optimization but incorporating its dynamics into a Kalman-style optimizer must remain computationally feasible for high-dimensional neural networks.
   method:  Augment the Kalman state with velocities p_k and define momentum-like dynamics x_k = x_{k-1} + p_{k-1} + w_k and p_k = κ p_{k-1} + u_k, while keeping the scalar-loss measurement h_k(x)=ˆL_k(x). Reformulate as a 2n-dimensional EKF and approximate the posterior covariance with a 2×2 block structure times identity, and approximate H_k^T H_k accordingly, yielding KOALA-M that captures momentum while retaining linear complexity in parameters. Adaptivity is further improved by online estimation of the measurement and state noise via exponential moving averages (e.g., β_R = 0.9, β_x = 0.9), reducing hyperparameter tuning and adjusting to minibatch-induced noise.

5. [ROBOTICS] Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation (2202.11742)
   problem: Behavior cloning for sequential policies suffers from train-test distribution shift, while reinforcement learning in VLN is difficult due to sparse rewards.
   method:  DUET is first pretrained with behavior cloning and auxiliary cross-modal tasks (masked language modeling, masked region classification, single-step action prediction, and object grounding) to initialize grounded representations. It is then fine-tuned with a pseudo interactive demonstrator (PID) akin to DAgger: during rollouts, a demonstrator with access to the full environment graph selects pseudo-target actions as navigable nodes minimizing the combined distance from the current node to the node and from the node to the goal. The policy is optimized with a balance between expert and pseudo labels, yielding stronger navigation performance than RL-based fine-tuning in experiments.

6. [ROBOTICS] Multiscale Sensor Fusion and Continuous Control with Neural CDEs (2203.08715)
   problem: Continuous-time CDE policies must be causal for real-time deployment, but natural cubic spline interpolation induces non-causal dependencies on future data.
   method:  Propose a discretely-online CDE evolution: output control at a fixed high rate and evolve the CDE over successive short intervals, where at each step the cubic spline is fit only to measurements within the current interval. This preserves causality at the control frequency while retaining smooth integration of high-rate signals between sparse images. For truly continuous-time control, a zero-order-hold interpolant can be used. This strategy enables practical, real-time deployment of CDE-driven policies without violating causality.

7. [IR_REC] Efficient Pointwise-Pairwise Learning-to-Rank for News Recommendation (2409.17711)
   problem: Pairwise and listwise inference can significantly improve ranking quality but are computationally prohibitive at inference time for large candidate sets.
   method:  GLIMPSE introduces an efficient rank aggregation strategy that initializes with a pointwise-ranked list and performs right-to-left (RTL) passes that only compare adjacent items among the top-k results, swapping when the pairwise model prefers the right item. Each RTL pass over k items requires just k−1 comparisons, enabling O(K) complexity per pass versus O(K^2) for all-pairs approaches. Hyperparameters control the number of RTL passes (m) and the top-k scope, delivering practical gains with very few pairwise calls while preserving scalability.

8. [CV] Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world (2212.00972)
   problem: Transferring the generalization of a large cloud model to a lightweight device model under continual shifts while keeping downlink bandwidth low and mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
   method:  Propose a Visual Prompt Learning Strategy with Uncertainty-Guided Updating (VPLU): learn pixel-level visual prompts added to input images (used in both cloud training and on-device inference) to pull the device test distribution closer to cloud training. Prompts are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA where the update weight increases with sample uncertainty, and are combined with adversarial feature alignment on the teacher (domain discriminator + GRL) and pseudo-label knowledge transfer to the student (high-confidence filtering). Only prompts (~0.4% of model parameters) and the lightweight student are sent to devices, yielding consistent gains (e.g., +3.3% over pseudo-label baseline; +2.7% from U-EMA) and alleviating forgetting across continual domains.

9. [ROBOTICS] TwinTac: A Wide-Range, Highly Sensitive Tactile Sensor with Real-to-Sim Digital Twin Sensor Model (2509.10063)
   problem: Non-optical tactile sensors often trade off sensitivity for range (or vice versa), limiting their usefulness in manipulation that spans delicate touch to high-force contacts.
   method:  PhysTac integrates eight MEMS pressure taxels (CPS135B-1500D) beneath an Ecoflex 00-31 elastomer, multiplexed over I2C (55 Hz) to achieve both ultra-high sensitivity and a wide pressure range. The sensor provides 0.01 kPa resolution over 50–1500 kPa, detects forces as low as 0.007 N and as high as 212 N, and exhibits a linear sensitivity of ~7.24 kPa/N. A radial basis function interpolation reconstructs a continuous spatial pressure map from the 8 taxels, enabling coarse shape discrimination despite the sparse array. (Supported: lines 60–75, 62–63, 138–140).

10. [RL] Inductive-bias-driven Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Schedules in Heterogeneous Clusters (1909.02119)
   problem: Adding a Bayesian model to guide RL typically requires full probabilistic inference during training, which is computationally prohibitive for periodic retraining in real systems.
   method:  The paper introduces a sampling-based gradient estimator for Bayesian networks that computes gradients of BN parameters without performing full posterior inference. By generating samples and expanding only the necessary BN subgraph, it recursively estimates gradients with favorable complexity (especially on polytree BNs), decoupling BN training from the inference procedure used to form the belief state. This accelerates learning, yielding about 4× faster training than belief-propagation-based full inference and ~2.2× better sample efficiency than a model-free RNN baseline.

11. [MULTIMODAL] Progress-Aware Video Frame Captioning (2412.02071)
   problem: Downstream video understanding and selection tasks lack temporally fine-grained supervision to pick informative frames and reason about ordered events.
   method:  Use ProgressCaptioner’s per-frame, progress-aware captions as an intermediate textual representation. An LLM compares consecutive captions to select keyframes that mark action progression, improving action recognition when replacing uniformly sampled frames. The same captions guide zero-shot frame-wise classification (via LLM multi-choice over labels) and enhance zero-shot video QA on NExT-QA (ATP-Hard), surpassing prior zero-shot baselines and improving temporal reasoning performance.

12. [ROBOTICS] Textile Taxonomy and Classification Using Pulling and Twisting (2103.09555)
   problem: Robotics lacks a structured textile taxonomy aligned with industry practice, making it hard to study and benchmark methods for identifying fabric properties relevant to manipulation.
   method:  The paper proposes a textile taxonomy that jointly considers fiber material (e.g., wool, polyester, cotton) and production method (woven vs. knitted). Textile classes are determined from microscope imagery of samples to capture construction details invisible to normal cameras. A dataset of 40 labeled samples is built according to this taxonomy, and analyses (t-SNE, SVM, CNN) show construction-based labeling yields clearer separability and substantially higher accuracy than material-only labels.

13. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Learning accurate spatialization from visuals is harder with multiple concurrent sources; models need stronger audio-visual disambiguation without relying on real binaural recordings.
   method:  Augment training with an auxiliary audio-visual source separation task using only mono data. Two sources are placed at opposite image edges in the pseudo visual input, and an APNet separation branch predicts individual STFTs (Sa, Sb) under a Mix-and-Separate setup while sharing the audio backbone with the stereo task. Joint training improves the model’s ability to associate visual locations with source-specific audio, boosting binaural generation performance.

14. [CV] Learning Predictive Models From Observation and Interaction (1912.12773)
   problem: Large domain shift between observation and interaction datasets (e.g., human vs. robot embodiments, different environments) prevents naively sharing action latents across domains.
   method:  Partition the latent z into shared and domain-specific components (z_shared, z_domain) and learn domain-dependent priors. Use a shared Gaussian prior for z_shared across domains, and separate learned Gaussian priors for z_domain in each domain; for human observation data, parameterize the z_domain prior temporally with an LSTM conditioned on the initial frame to capture correlated human actions. This domain-dependent prior enables leveraging human and dashboard videos to improve robot/driving action-conditioned prediction and downstream planning.

15. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Supervised stereo models often overfit to datasets and existing evaluation metrics underweight phase errors that determine perceived direction.
   method:  Use pseudo visual-stereo pairs as data augmentation (Augment-PseudoBinaural) alongside real binaural recordings to improve supervised training, yielding consistent gains across FAIR-Play and MUSIC-Stereo. Additionally, propose Difference Phase Distance (D_phase), a phase-sensitive metric computed on the difference spectrum between left and right channels (comparing the phase of SD and the predicted M*Sm), making evaluation more aligned with directional perception.

16. [LLM_NLP] BioDEX: Large-Scale Biomedical Adverse Drug Event Extraction for Real-World Pharmacovigilance (2305.13395)
   problem: PV literature triage often relies on naive keyword lookups for reactions/drugs, yielding low precision when identifying papers that truly report ADEs for targets of interest.
   method:  The authors repurpose model-extracted reactions/drugs to perform per-paper binary classification (e.g., whether a reaction is part of an ADE), demonstrating higher macro-F1 than lookup baselines across the 30 most frequent reactions (and similarly for drugs). This suggests the extractor can prioritize relevant papers more effectively in real PV workflows.

17. [CV] Hit-Detector: Hierarchical Trinity Architecture Search for Object Detection (2003.11818)
   problem: Directly searching a very large, shared operation space across all detector components is computationally prohibitive and inefficient because different parts (backbone, neck, head) prefer different operators.
   method:  Propose hierarchical screening to automatically derive tailored sub search spaces per component using column-sparse (group sparsity) regularization over the architecture-parameter matrix to rank and prune operation candidates. Sequentially filter from a 32-op global space down to compact per-part subspaces (e.g., Nb=Nn=Nh=8), then run the end-to-end differentiable search within these tailored spaces. Ablations show that using three specialized subspaces improves mAP from 40.1% (single shared subspace) to 41.4%, and the sparsity term (µ≈0.1) stabilizes and sharpens operation selection.

18. [ROBOTICS] Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills (1911.00969)
   problem: Skills learned with fixtures often fail once the fixtures are removed; naive removal causes large performance drops.
   method:  Replace hard fixture constraints by a soft, repulsive potential field that matches the fixture geometry inferred from depth, and gradually move the physical fixture away while continuing to train the inner-loop policy initialized from the fixture-trained policy. On peg insertion, this curriculum recovers performance to about 70% success when the fixture is fully removed, versus around 10% with naive removal, maintaining the task skill without physical scaffolding.

19. [MULTIMODAL] VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection (2411.14794)
   problem: Reasoning over videos with LVLMs is computationally expensive due to many redundant frames and long contexts, and models often lack structured, evidence-driven reasoning.
   method:  Propose Hybrid LVLMs Collaboration for VideoQA: a lightweight Frame Selector followed by a fine-grained reasoning LVLM. A tiny LVLM (~1B) captions sampled frames and a tiny LLM (~0.5–1.5B) selects question-relevant core frames; the reasoning LVLM is trained with a two-stage SFT—stage-1 generates multimodal evidence and stage-2 answers using that evidence. At inference, the selector reduces inputs to around 2–3 core frames, then the LVLM performs CoT-based reasoning, yielding higher accuracy and substantially lower TFLOPs on a 14-task benchmark; the selector is plug-and-play and improves efficiency across different LVLMs (e.g., GPT-4o, InternVL2).

20. [CV] Latent Discriminant Deterministic Uncertainty (2207.10130)
   problem: Deterministic uncertainty methods often rely on bi-Lipschitz constraints that hinder scalability and stability on complex, high-resolution vision tasks (e.g., semantic segmentation), while ensemble/sampling methods are computationally expensive.
   method:  Introduce LDU, which inserts a distinction maximization (DM) layer with an arbitrary number of trainable prototypes into the pre-logit latent space and measures prototype similarity via cosine similarity, followed by an exponential activation to sharpen responses. This learns a discriminative latent space without enforcing Lipschitz constraints, enabling single forward-pass uncertainty estimation that scales to classification, semantic segmentation, and depth regression. An auxiliary MLP head g_unc operates on the DM embedding to output a confidence score. Experiments show competitive uncertainty performance with Deep Ensembles across tasks at much lower computational cost.

21. [LLM_NLP] MotivGraph-SoIQ: Integrating Motivational Knowledge Graphs and Socratic Dialogue for Enhanced LLM Ideation (2509.21978)
   problem: LLM-generated research ideas often lack rigorous motivational grounding in the literature due to probabilistic internal knowledge and limited context, leading to superficial or infeasible proposals.
   method:  Introduce MotivGraph, a motivational knowledge graph that encodes research motivations as (problem, challenge, solution) triples with explicit edge types (parent-of, problem–challenge, challenge–solution). Build it automatically via SciMotivMiner, which extracts standardized, author-agnostic triples from papers and merges duplicates, then add a hierarchy using a Parent Node Addition algorithm that embeds nodes, finds nearest neighbors, and leverages an LLM to form general parent nodes. During ideation, agents use graph tools (fuzzy node search and relation retrieval) to ground ideas on concrete nodes from the graph, supplying structured, cross-linked motivational context for each proposal.

1. Learning to Adapt Frozen CLIP for Few-Shot Test-Time Domain Adaptation (International Conference on Learning Representations 2025, cites: 13)
   Few-shot Test-Time Domain Adaptation focuses on adapting a model at test time to a specific domain using only a few unlabeled examples, addressing domain shift. Prior methods leverage CLIP's strong out-of-distribution (OOD) abilities by generating domain-specific prompts to guide its generalized, frozen features. However, since downstream datasets are not explicitly seen by CLIP, solely depending on the feature space knowledge is constrained by CLIP's prior knowledge. Notably, when using a less robust backbone like ViT-B/16, performance significantly drops on challenging real-world benchmarks. Departing from the state-of-the-art of inheriting the intrinsic OOD capability of CLIP, this work introduces learning directly on the input space to complement the dataset-specific knowledge for frozen CLIP. Specifically, an independent side branch is attached in parallel with CLIP and enforced to learn exclusive knowledge via revert attention. To better capture the dataset-specific label semantics for downstream adaptation, we propose to enhance the inter-dispersion among text features via greedy text ensemble and refinement. The text and visual features are then progressively fused in a domain-aware manner by a generated domain prompt to adapt toward a specific domain. Extensive experiments show our method's superiority on 5 large-scale benchmarks (WILDS and DomainNet), notably improving over smaller networks like ViT-B/16 with gains of \textbf{+5.1} in F1 for iWildCam and \textbf{+3.1\%} in WC Acc for FMoW.

2. SCAP: Transductive Test-Time Adaptation via Supportive Clique-based Attribute Prompting (Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2025, cites: 9)
   Vision-language models (VLMs) encounter considerable challenges when adapting to domain shifts stemming from changes in data distribution. Test-time adaptation (TTA) has emerged as a promising approach to enhance VLM performance under such conditions. In practice, test data often arrives in batches, leading to increasing interest in the transductive TTA setting. However, existing TTA methods primarily focus on individual test samples, overlooking crucial cross-sample correlations within a batch. While recent ViT-based TTA methods have introduced batch-level adaptation, they remain suboptimal for VLMs due to inadequate integration of the text modality. To address these limitations, we propose a novel transductive TTA framework, Supportive Clique-based Attribute Prompting (SCAP), which effectively combines visual and textual information to enhance adaptation by generating fine-grained attribute prompts across test batches. SCAP first forms supportive cliques of test samples in an unsupervised manner based on visual similarity and learns an attribute prompt for each clique, capturing shared attributes critical for adaptation. For each test sample, SCAP aggregates attribute prompts from its associated cliques, providing enriched contextual information. To ensure adaptability over time, we incorporate a retention module that dynamically updates attribute prompts and their associated attributes as new data arrives. Comprehensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that SCAP outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, significantly advancing VLM generalization under domain shifts. Our code is available at https://github.com/zhoujiahuan1991/CVPR2025-SCAP.

3. Multi-Scale Global-Instance Prompt Tuning for Continual Test-Time Adaptation in Medical Image Segmentation (IEEE International Conference on Bioinformatics and Biomedicine 2025, cites: 0)
   Distribution shift is a common challenge in medical images obtained from different clinical centers, significantly hindering the deployment of pre-trained semantic segmentation models in real-world applications across multiple domains. Continual Test-Time Adaptation (CTTA) has emerged as a promising approach to address cross-domain distribution shifts during continually evolving target domains. Most existing CTTA methods rely on incrementally updating model parameters, which inevitably suffer from error accumulation and catastrophic forgetting, especially in long-term adaptation. Recent prompt-tuning-based works have shown potential to mitigate the two issues above by updating only visual prompts. While these approaches have demonstrated promising performance, several limitations remain: 1) lacking multi-scale prompt diversity, 2) inadequate incorporation of instance-specific knowledge, and 3) risk of privacy leakage. To overcome these limitations, we propose Multi-scale Global-Instance Prompt Tuning (MGIPT), to enhance scale diversity of prompts as well as capture both globaland instance-level knowledge for robust CTTA. Specifically, MGIPT consists of an Adaptive-scale Instance Prompt (AIP) and a Multi-scale Global-level Prompt (MGP). AIP dynamically learns lightweight and instance-specific prompts to mitigate error accumulation with adaptive optimal-scale selection mechanism. MGP captures domain-level knowledge across different scales to ensure robust adaptation with anti-forgetting capabilities. These complementary components are combined through a weighted ensemble approach, enabling effective dual-level adaptation that integrates both global and local information. Extensive experiments on medical image segmentation benchmarks (five optic disc/cup datasets and four polyp datasets) demonstrate that our MGIPT outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving robust adaptation across continually changing target domains. Notably, our MGIPT exhibits particularly strong performance in longterm CTTA scenarios, showing great anti-forgetting ability.

4. BaFTA: Backprop-Free Test-Time Adaptation For Zero-Shot Vision-Language Models (arXiv.org 2024, cites: 3)
   Large-scale pretrained vision-language models like CLIP have demonstrated remarkable zero-shot image classification capabilities across diverse domains. To enhance CLIP's performance while preserving the zero-shot paradigm, various test-time prompt tuning methods have been introduced to refine class embeddings through unsupervised learning objectives during inference. However, these methods often encounter challenges in selecting appropriate learning rates to prevent collapsed training in the absence of validation data during test-time adaptation. In this study, we propose a novel backpropagation-free algorithm BaFTA for test-time adaptation of vision-language models. Instead of fine-tuning text prompts to refine class embeddings, our approach directly estimates class centroids using online clustering within a projected embedding space that aligns text and visual embeddings. We dynamically aggregate predictions from both estimated and original class embeddings, as well as from distinct augmented views, by assessing the reliability of each prediction using R\'enyi Entropy. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that BaFTA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art test-time adaptation methods in both effectiveness and efficiency.

5. Test-Time Adaptation of a Multi-Class Object Localization and Size Estimation Framework for Smart Agriculture Applications (Cognitive Computation 2025, cites: 0)
   <no abstract>

6. WATT: Weight Average Test-Time Adaptation of CLIP (Neural Information Processing Systems 2024, cites: 30)
   Vision-Language Models (VLMs) such as CLIP have yielded unprecedented performance for zero-shot image classification, yet their generalization capability may still be seriously challenged when confronted to domain shifts. In response, we present Weight Average Test-Time Adaptation (WATT) of CLIP, a pioneering approach facilitating full test-time adaptation (TTA) of this VLM. Our method employs a diverse set of templates for text prompts, augmenting the existing framework of CLIP. Predictions are utilized as pseudo labels for model updates, followed by weight averaging to consolidate the learned information globally. Furthermore, we introduce a text ensemble strategy, enhancing overall test performance by aggregating diverse textual cues. Our findings underscore the efficacy of WATT in enhancing performance across diverse datasets, including CIFAR-10-C, CIFAR-10.1, CIFAR-100-C, VisDA-C, and several other challenging datasets, effectively covering a wide range of domain shifts. Notably, these enhancements are achieved without necessitating additional model transformations or trainable modules. Moreover, compared to other Test-Time Adaptation methods, our approach can operate effectively with just a single image. Highlighting the potential of innovative test-time strategies, this research emphasizes their role in fortifying the adaptability of VLMs. The implementation is available at: \url{https://github.com/Mehrdad-Noori/WATT.git}.

7. DART: Dual-Modal Adaptive Online Prompting and Knowledge Retention for Test-Time Adaptation (AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence 2024, cites: 14)
   As an up-and-coming area, CLIP-based pre-trained vision-language models can readily facilitate downstream tasks through the zero-shot or few-shot fine-tuning manners. However, they still face critical challenges in test-time generalization due to the shifts between the training and test data distributions, hindering the further improvement of the performance. To address this crucial problem, the latest works have introduced Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) techniques to CLIP which dynamically learn text prompts using only test samples. However, their limited learning capacity due to the overlook of visual modality information, and the underutilization of knowledge in previously seen test samples result in reduced performance. In this paper, we propose a novel Dual-modal Adaptive online prompting and knowledge ReTention method called DART to overcome these challenges. To increase the learning capacity, DART captures knowledge from each test sample by learning class-specific text prompts and instance-level image prompts. Additionally, to fully leverage the knowledge from previously seen test samples, DART utilizes dual-modal knowledge retention prompts to adaptively retain the acquired knowledge, thereby enhancing the predictions on subsequent test samples. Extensive experiments on various large-scale benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed DART against state-of-the-art methods.

8. Training-Free Test-Time Adaptation with Brownian Distance Covariance in Vision-Language Models (IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing 2026, cites: 0)
   Vision-language models suffer performance degradation under domain shift, limiting real-world applicability. Existing test-time adaptation methods are computationally intensive, rely on back-propagation, and often focus on single modalities. To address these issues, we propose Training-free Test-Time Adaptation with Brownian Distance Covariance (TaTa). TaTa leverages Brownian Distance Covariance-a powerful statistical measure that captures both linear and nonlinear dependencies via pairwise distances-to dynamically adapt VLMs to new domains without training or back-propagation. This not only improves efficiency but also enhances stability by avoiding disruptive weight updates. TaTa further integrates attribute-enhanced prompting to improve vision-language inference with descriptive visual cues. Combined with dynamic clustering and pseudo-label refinement, it effectively recalibrates the model for novel visual contexts. Experiments across diverse datasets show that TaTa significantly reduces computational cost while achieving state-of-the-art performance in domain and cross-dataset generalization.

9. Is Less More? Exploring Token Condensation as Training-free Test-time Adaptation ( 2024, cites: 5)
   Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP) excels at learning generalizable image representations but often falls short in zero-shot inference on certain downstream datasets. Test-time adaptation (TTA) mitigates this issue by adjusting components like normalization layers or context prompts, yet it typically requires large batch sizes and extensive augmentations, leading to high computational costs. This raises a key question: Can VLMs' performance drop in specific test cases be mitigated through efficient, training-free approaches? To explore the solution, we investigate token condensation (TC) techniques, originally designed to enhance vision transformer efficiency by refining token usage during inference. We observe that informative tokens improve visual-text alignment in VLMs like CLIP on unseen datasets. However, existing TC methods often fail to maintain in-distribution performance when reducing tokens, prompting us to ask: How can we transform TC into an effective ``free-lunch'' adaptation strategy for VLMs? To address this, we propose Token Condensation as Adaptation (TCA), a training-free adaptation method that takes a step beyond standard TC. Rather than passively discarding tokens, TCA condenses token representation by introducing reservoir-based domain anchor tokens for information-preserving token reduction and logits correction. TCA achieves up to a 21.4% performance improvement over the strongest baseline on cross-dataset benchmark and the CIFAR-100-Corrupted dataset while reducing GFLOPs by 12.2% to 48.9%, with minimal hyperparameter dependency on both CLIP and SigLIP series.

10. PatAug: Augmentation of Augmentation for Test-Time Adaptation (ACM Multimedia 2025, cites: 1)
   The rich pretrained knowledge in vision-language models (VLMs) endows them with the ability to discriminate common objects given only category names, but may be challenged by out-of-distribution unlabeled samples. To address this limitation, test-time adaptation (TTA) dynamically adjusts VLMs to target distributions during inference. Current TTA frameworks rely heavily on unsupervised data augmentations to enhance sample informativeness, but remain vulnerable to naive augmented views. This work introduces Patch Augmentation (PatAug), a pixel-level perturbation framework that optimizes the benefits of informative augmentations and mitigates negative transformation impacts. Implemented as trainable pixels, PatAug are prepared given only category names before inference, introducing few additional overheads. The patches encode class-related semantic information. They assist VLMs in emphasizing on the compatible visual information in the images, restoring perturbed image details, while retaining unrecognized information. Such merits inspire the design of an augmentation of augmentation framework, where PatAug is applied to standard augmentation views for reliable TTA inference results. To better fit the target distributions, we adjust patches with a cross-modal similarity alignment loss and learnable patching weights. Experiments on natural and specialized domain shifts confirm the effectiveness of PatAug.

1. NeuroCognitor: Unified EEG-Language Framework for Cognitive Load Analysis via Instruction-Tuned Multi-Task Learning (IEEE Access 2025, cites: 1)
   Cognitive load estimation from electroencephalography (EEG) is pivotal to safety-critical domains where mental state monitoring is essential, such as healthcare, education, aviation, and human–computer interaction, yet prevailing deep models remain 1) task-specific and brittle across datasets; 2) weakly aligned to language semantics; and 3) sensitive to inter-/intra-subject variability and noise. Existing EEG foundation models also lack a pathway to instruction-tuned multi-task reasoning because semantically aligned EEG–text pairs are scarce. There is no unified framework that 1) discretises EEG into language-compatible tokens; 2) aligns their latent distributions with text without paired supervision; and 3) supports instruction-driven generalisation across heterogeneous EEG tasks. To address these challenges, we propose a unified EEG–language (NeuroCognitor) framework that 1) performs modality-aware vector-quantised tokenisation of time–frequency EEG segments, 2) enforces distributional cross-modal alignment via adversarial learning with gradient reversal, and 3) pretrains a multi-stream autoregressive prior with a stair-stepping causal mask before instruction-tuned multi-task adaptation on a causal LLM. On three diverse datasets, NeuroCognitor attains competitive performance and strong cross-task generalisability: on TUAB abnormality detection, NeuroCognitor-L achieves balanced accuracy 0.83 and AUROC 0.92; on TUEV event classification, it reaches Cohen’s $\kappa =0.71$ and weighted F $1~0.88$ . Ablations confirm the benefits of codebook size (~8,192 entries), distributional alignment over fragment-level pairing, tokenization granularity, and strict temporal causal masking. Casting EEG as discrete tokens in a shared vocabulary with text enables a single instruction-following model to support abnormality screening, event detection, and sleep staging, thereby reducing per-task engineering and maintenance. Pair-free EEG–text alignment lowers annotation burden and facilitates deployment in data-sparse settings. Cross-dataset robustness and compatibility with standard 10–20/10–10 montages improve portability across sites and devices, while the tokenized interface provides auditable, integration-ready outputs for decision support and human–machine monitoring workflows.

1. Test-Time Adaptation via Cache Personalization for Facial Expression Recognition in Videos ( 2026, cites: 1)
   Facial expression recognition (FER) in videos requires model personalization to capture the considerable variations across subjects. Vision-language models (VLMs) offer strong transfer to downstream tasks through image-text alignment, but their performance can still degrade under inter-subject distribution shifts. Personalizing models using test-time adaptation (TTA) methods can mitigate this challenge. However, most state-of-the-art TTA methods rely on unsupervised parameter optimization, introducing computational overhead that is impractical in many real-world applications. This paper introduces TTA through Cache Personalization (TTA-CaP), a cache-based TTA method that enables cost-effective (gradient-free) personalization of VLMs for video FER. Prior cache-based TTA methods rely solely on dynamic memories that store test samples, which can accumulate errors and drift due to noisy pseudo-labels. TTA-CaP leverages three coordinated caches: a personalized source cache that stores source-domain prototypes, a positive target cache that accumulates reliable subject-specific samples, and a negative target cache that stores low-confidence cases as negative samples to reduce the impact of noisy pseudo-labels. Cache updates and replacement are controlled by a tri-gate mechanism based on temporal stability, confidence, and consistency with the personalized cache. Finally, TTA-CaP refines predictions through fusion of embeddings, yielding refined representations that support temporally stable video-level predictions. Our experiments on three challenging video FER datasets, BioVid, StressID, and BAH, indicate that TTA-CaP can outperform state-of-the-art TTA methods under subject-specific and environmental shifts, while maintaining low computational and memory overhead for real-world deployment.

In particular, we propose generating pseudo-labels for the noisy test-time samples by aligning visual embeddings with reliable, text-based semantic anchors. Specifically, to maintain the regular structure of the dataset properly, we formulate the problem as a batch-wise label assignment, which is efficiently solved using Optimal Transport. Our method, Semantic Anchor Transport (SAT), utilizes such pseudo-labels as supervisory signals for test-time adaptation, yielding a principled cross-modal alignment solution. Moreover, SAT further leverages heterogeneous textual clues, with a multi-template distillation approach that replicates multi-view contrastive learning strategies in unsupervised representation learning without incurring additional computational complexity. Extensive experiments on multiple popular test-time adaptation benchmarks presenting diverse complexity empirically show the superiority of SAT, achieving consistent performance gains over recent state-of-the-art methods, yet being computationally efficient.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Vision-language models trained on web data degrade when images
come from a shifted distribution like medical or satellite;
how can we maintain cross-modal alignment under unseen domain
shift without target-domain labels?


### Proposed method
We propose COMPASS, a test-time and continual, label-free adaptation layer that preserves vision–language alignment under unseen domain shift (e.g., medical, satellite) without target-domain labels. Starting from a frozen web-trained VLM (e.g., CLIP-like), we keep the text encoder fixed and adapt only a small set of vision-side modules: (i) a lightweight visual prompt front-end (pixel-level or early-feature prompts) and (ii) a factorized adapter that decomposes the visual representation into a shared, alignment-preserving pathway and a domain-specific modulation pathway. An exponential-moving-average (EMA) teacher of the full model anchors the original cross-modal geometry and prevents drift.

The domain-binding front-end couples two mechanisms. First, a learnable visual prompt bank is injected before or into the first few vision blocks, nudging inputs from the target domain toward the web pretraining manifold. Second, an amortized inference head estimates a per-image domain code z_dom that modulates intermediate features via a small FiLM/LoRA adapter stack, while a shared pathway passes gradients only through the adapters and prompts (the backbone remains frozen). We place a simple Gaussian prior over z_dom and regularize with a KL term so the domain-specific modulation remains compact and does not entangle with the shared alignment space. Both prompts and adapters are updated online, while the z_dom prior is refined slowly across the stream.

Unsupervised alignment is maintained with three complementary signals. (1) Self-captioned contrastive alignment: for each target image, the VLM (or a small caption head) generates K candidate captions; we keep high-confidence text anchors and optimize an InfoNCE loss to increase image–text similarity for the top caption and decrease it for hard negatives (including other candidates and a fixed bank of generic web prompts). (2) Cross-view consistency: two strong augmentations of the same image must produce similar similarities over the caption bank, stabilizing the image embedding without labels. (3) Entropy shaping: we minimize entropy of the image–text similarity distribution over the candidate bank to produce confident, clustered alignments while avoiding collapse with temperature and teacher constraints.

To avoid catastrophic forgetting and preserve cross-modal geometry, we use a teacher–student consistency term: the student’s similarity matrix over a small replay buffer of web images and a fixed set of web text anchors is matched to the EMA teacher. We further add a lightweight domain discriminator on shared visual features with a gradient-reversal layer to keep the shared pathway domain-invariant; the domain-specific modulation absorbs style/content shift. Updates are uncertainty-weighted: per-sample confidence scores downweight noisy self-captions and limit prompt/adapter drift. Only prompts, adapters, z_dom inference, and the domain discriminator are trained; the text encoder, visual backbone, and the EMA teacher’s main weights remain frozen.

Deployment: as target images stream in, we run a few inner-loop updates per batch with small learning rates and an uncertainty-guided EMA on the prompt bank. We cap compute by freezing deeper adapters, and apply early stopping when teacher–student discrepancy stops improving. At inference, the adapted prompts/adapters are used with the frozen text encoder; no target labels or paired texts are required, and the method remains open-vocabulary for downstream zero-shot queries.

### Rationale
Cross-modal degradation under domain shift stems from visual features drifting away from the text-aligned manifold. By freezing the text encoder and constraining adaptation to small visual prompts and domain-specific adapters, we retain the shared alignment while granting just enough capacity to absorb style/content shift. The domain-factorized design explicitly separates a stable, alignment-preserving pathway from a compact domain modulation controlled by a prior, reducing the chance that adaptation breaks the learned image–text geometry.

Because no labels are available, we rely on self-captioning and open-vocabulary text banks to create pseudo text anchors, but guard against error amplification with uncertainty-weighted updates, cross-view consistency, and teacher–student constraints on a web replay buffer. Adversarial alignment further nudges shared features toward domain invariance, while uncertainty-aware prompt updates keep compute low and adaptation stable. Together, these ingredients maintain cross-modal alignment in new domains without target supervision.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [IR_REC] GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation (2007.02747)
   problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
   method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

2. [IR_REC] Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition (2505.07166)
   problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
   method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

3. [LLM_NLP] Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement (2402.11436)
   problem: LLM self-refine and self-rewarding pipelines can optimize toward falsely perceived improvements due to self-evaluation bias, causing quality to stagnate or degrade.
   method:  Empirically compare LLM self-evaluations against reference-based BLEURT and human MQM across six LLMs, four languages, and three tasks, showing universal self-bias that amplifies over iterations; in self-rewarding, increasing the candidate pool size further amplifies bias. Analyze what changes after self-refine with UniEval and style-paraphrasing experiments, finding perceived gains are mainly in fluency/understandability and style alignment rather than actual task objectives, explaining the divergence between self-estimated and true performance.

4. [CV] KOALA: A Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity (2107.03331)
   problem: Momentum improves optimization but incorporating its dynamics into a Kalman-style optimizer must remain computationally feasible for high-dimensional neural networks.
   method:  Augment the Kalman state with velocities p_k and define momentum-like dynamics x_k = x_{k-1} + p_{k-1} + w_k and p_k = κ p_{k-1} + u_k, while keeping the scalar-loss measurement h_k(x)=ˆL_k(x). Reformulate as a 2n-dimensional EKF and approximate the posterior covariance with a 2×2 block structure times identity, and approximate H_k^T H_k accordingly, yielding KOALA-M that captures momentum while retaining linear complexity in parameters. Adaptivity is further improved by online estimation of the measurement and state noise via exponential moving averages (e.g., β_R = 0.9, β_x = 0.9), reducing hyperparameter tuning and adjusting to minibatch-induced noise.

5. [ROBOTICS] Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation (2202.11742)
   problem: Behavior cloning for sequential policies suffers from train-test distribution shift, while reinforcement learning in VLN is difficult due to sparse rewards.
   method:  DUET is first pretrained with behavior cloning and auxiliary cross-modal tasks (masked language modeling, masked region classification, single-step action prediction, and object grounding) to initialize grounded representations. It is then fine-tuned with a pseudo interactive demonstrator (PID) akin to DAgger: during rollouts, a demonstrator with access to the full environment graph selects pseudo-target actions as navigable nodes minimizing the combined distance from the current node to the node and from the node to the goal. The policy is optimized with a balance between expert and pseudo labels, yielding stronger navigation performance than RL-based fine-tuning in experiments.

6. [ROBOTICS] Multiscale Sensor Fusion and Continuous Control with Neural CDEs (2203.08715)
   problem: Continuous-time CDE policies must be causal for real-time deployment, but natural cubic spline interpolation induces non-causal dependencies on future data.
   method:  Propose a discretely-online CDE evolution: output control at a fixed high rate and evolve the CDE over successive short intervals, where at each step the cubic spline is fit only to measurements within the current interval. This preserves causality at the control frequency while retaining smooth integration of high-rate signals between sparse images. For truly continuous-time control, a zero-order-hold interpolant can be used. This strategy enables practical, real-time deployment of CDE-driven policies without violating causality.

7. [IR_REC] Efficient Pointwise-Pairwise Learning-to-Rank for News Recommendation (2409.17711)
   problem: Pairwise and listwise inference can significantly improve ranking quality but are computationally prohibitive at inference time for large candidate sets.
   method:  GLIMPSE introduces an efficient rank aggregation strategy that initializes with a pointwise-ranked list and performs right-to-left (RTL) passes that only compare adjacent items among the top-k results, swapping when the pairwise model prefers the right item. Each RTL pass over k items requires just k−1 comparisons, enabling O(K) complexity per pass versus O(K^2) for all-pairs approaches. Hyperparameters control the number of RTL passes (m) and the top-k scope, delivering practical gains with very few pairwise calls while preserving scalability.

Prompts are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA where the update weight increases with sample uncertainty, and are combined with adversarial feature alignment on the teacher (domain discriminator + GRL) and pseudo-label knowledge transfer to the student (high-confidence filtering).    problem: Adding a Bayesian model to guide RL typically requires full probabilistic inference during training, which is computationally prohibitive for periodic retraining in real systems.

By generating samples and expanding only the necessary BN subgraph, it recursively estimates gradients with favorable complexity (especially on polytree BNs), decoupling BN training from the inference procedure used to form the belief state. 20. [CV] Latent Discriminant Deterministic Uncertainty (2207.10130)
   problem: Deterministic uncertainty methods often rely on bi-Lipschitz constraints that hinder scalability and stability on complex, high-resolution vision tasks (e.g., semantic segmentation), while ensemble/sampling methods are computationally expensive.
   method:  Introduce LDU, which inserts a distinction maximization (DM) layer with an arbitrary number of trainable prototypes into the pre-logit latent space and measures prototype similarity via cosine similarity, followed by an exponential activation to sharpen responses.
